items.image: images/Chicago Skyline A Fluid Transcription,7x 79 in,Ink and watercolor on rice paper,2025.jpg
items.title: Chicago Skyline: A Fluid Transcription
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Ink and watercolor on rice paper (Scroll)  Size: 7 x 79 in
items.description: I approach the Chicago skyline not as a fortress of steel, but as a rhythmic, atmospheric vibration. Painted on rice paper, the work abandons architectural rigidity for intuitive, fluid gestures. I utilize the paper\'s absorbency to let water-heavy blue pigments bleed, softening hard geometry into abstract verticality. Repetitive grids and washing tones do not merely depict facades; they capture the cold, melancholic breath of the city viewed from a distance. The scroll format introduces a temporal dimension, unfolding the view not as a static snapshot, but as a continuous, drifting narrative. By relinquishing tight control, I allow the medium to dictate form, transforming the external landscape into an internal psychological space---a fleeting memory of the city rather than a document.

items.image: images/The Subconscious River,128x11 in,Ink&mixed media on paper,2025.jpg
items.title: The Subconscious River
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Ink and mixed media on paper (Scroll)  Size: 128x11 in
items.description: Black ink crashes into gold. Chaos spreads across the long paper. I lost control on purpose. Growing up with traditional Chinese scrolls, I was taught to see nature as a balanced narrative. But my internal world is not balanced at all. So I poured fluid ink to map my own psychological anxiety. The scroll format forces you to read the panic over time. It is a timeline of a mind losing its grip on the physical world.

items.title: Reflection / Duality
items.meta: Year: 2024  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size: 18 x 24 in
items.description:
In this monochromatic work, I strip away the distraction of color to confront the self in a void of crimson and oxblood. I am attempting to capture the heavy, visceral weight of the gaze. Here, the mirror is not merely an optical tool for verifying appearance, but a psychological threshold where the unified self fractures. By locking eyes with the reflection, I engage in a silent interrogation of identity, exploring the feeling of jamais vu---the unsettling moment when one's own face becomes unrecognizable. The choice of red is deliberate; it evokes a biological interiority, blurring the line between the external space and the blood running beneath the skin. I built up the paint with dense, deliberate strokes to give the atmosphere a suffocating

physicality. Through this piece, I seek to visualize the anxiety of isolation, questioning whether the reflection confirms my existence or reveals the inherent instability of the "I."

items.title: Sensory Wash
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size: 20 x 28 in
items.description:
This painting represents my inquiry into the porosity of the human form. I am interested in the fleeting moment where the defensive barrier of the skin softens, allowing the external world to merge with the internal self. I overlay a realistic, grounded rendering of the figure with abstract, fluid lines---suggesting currents of sound, water, or emotion that wash over the body. Rather than penetrating or wounding the flesh, these elements envelope it, creating a dialogue between the solidity of the physical form and the intangibility of sensation. I painted the overlay with loose, intuitive gestures to contrast with the stillness of the face. I am trying to visualize a state of total immersion and surrender. In the context of \"Seeing Through,\" this

work proposes that true perception requires a dissolution of boundaries, asking us to acknowledge that we are not separate from the world, but permeable to it.

items.title: The Weight of White
items.meta: Year: 2024 Medium: Oil on canvas Size: 36 x 24 in
items.description: White fabric wraps around her head. Heavy and entirely still. I painted this to figure out how cultural memory actually feels. Moving from Beijing to Chicago changes your internal gravity. I used thick blue strokes to anchor her to the canvas. She is caught in a quiet, suspended state. Does our cultural identity protect us, or does it weigh us down? I am mapping that exact psychological hesitation.

items.title: The Golden Boundary
items.meta: **Year:** 2025  **Medium:** Oil on canvas  **Size:** 18 x 22 in
items.description: She turns her back to the viewer. Flat, golden patterns start to swallow her space. I took Gustav Klimt's decorative language and forced it into a modern clash. The realistic, breathing flesh fights against the rigid 2D geometry. We constantly build beautiful surfaces to hide our messy realities. I want to see which visual system wins. Will the pattern completely erase her human identity?

items.title: Spheres of the Feline Spirit: A Pluralistic Vision
items.meta: Year: 2024  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size:18 x 24 in
items.description:
I approach the feline form not as a singular organism, but as a vibrating accumulation of cosmic spheres. Painted with heavy oil pigments, the work abandons traditional biological realism for a pluralistic, geometric language. I utilize the medium's inherent impasto to sculpt each individual circle, transforming the flat canvas into a tactile field of depth and light. The sharp, complementary contrast between the atmospheric teals and the earth-bound oranges does not merely delineate space; it captures the internal tension between the unknown and the grounded.

The emerald-green eyes act as a gravitational anchor, holding the drifting fragments of form in a state of permanent, watchful equilibrium. By fragmenting the face into these discrete, textured cells, I allow the medium to dictate a new kind of order, transforming a domestic subject into a psychological landscape—a study of how identity is forged from a multitude of independent energies rather than a static document of a creature.

items.title: Internal Bloom
items.meta: Year: 2024  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size: 36 x 24 in
items.description: A red heart burns inside a cage of blue bones. I cut the body open. Not with a knife, but with paint. I want to show the exact place where biological life meets emotional pain. The girl floats in a fluid, dreamlike space. So I exposed her ribs to break the defensive shell of the human form. I am digging into the vulnerability hidden under our meat. We hide our most vital organs behind bone. I forced them out into the open air.

items.title: Turn a Blind Eye
items.meta: Year: 2022 Medium magazine collage Size 14*17in
items.description: One eye is open to see the positive things in life and one eye is closed to hide from the negative. We often care about wealth and prosperity and are indifferent toward the dark side of society, avoiding them at all costs.

items.title: Through the Lens
items.meta: Year: 2022 Medium:wooden board, acrylic, water color, rice paper Size 18*22in
items.description:
Misinformation on the internet negatively
and unconsciously impacts our thinking. The things we see through the internet
are filtered and polished, but people often choose to believe before thinking.
Through this piece, I wish to advocate for others to see the world with their
own eyes.

items.title: Knot
items.meta: Year: 2022 Medium ink, watercolor, rice pa Size 20in*24in
items.description:
Practicing Chinese calligraphy was a big
part of my childhood; influenced by my roots, I combined elements from Chinese
ink painting and elements from western art to create a contrast.

items.image: images/The Book of Nightmares,8x6x0.6 in,Ink on paper, thread, pop-up book,2024.jpg
items.title: The Book of Nightmares(Click to view full pages)
items.meta: Year: 2026  Medium: Ink on paper, thread, pop-up book  Size: 21 x 15 x 1.5 cm (Closed)
items.description: The fear jumps straight out of the page. Literally. I built a physical trap out of paper and black ink. You look at the closed cover. It promises 21 by 15 centimeters of quiet safety. Then you pull it open. Sharp, broken geometric fragments violently construct a 3D architecture right in front of your eyes. I painted these scattered squares using traditional Chinese ink washes. I am forcing my formless nighttime anxiety to take up real physical volume. We expect sleep to provide a flat, restful plane. So what do you do when your subconscious attacks the waking world?

items.meta: Year: 2022 Medium glass, clay, acrylic, yarn,glitter Size 20*15*15in
items.title: Light up
items.description:
Glass is transparent, yet
blurred by the reflections which represent my inner world and the ethereal
distance between people. I used navy clothes to represent the universe, and the
floating glass cubes to represent the wandering lonely souls awaiting warmth.

items.title: Psychological Spill:The Fractured Internal Landscape
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size: 12 x 12 in
items.description: A bright yellow sphere burns in the corner. Reality fractures right there. I paint the exact second the external world melts into a dark psychological void. Cold, concrete geometry collapses completely. A dark shape covered in blue patterns spills across the jagged architecture. I am forcing the internal anxiety of isolation out into the physical room. The walls just cannot hold the mind anymore.

items.title: The Psychological Thermometer(Triptych / View All Panels)
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Oil on canvas (Triptych)  Size: 11x14 in,8x10 in,9x12in
items.description: Blue. Red. Green. I paint faces like weather reports. The flesh is a lie. It is just a surface to map internal temperature. I shoved these three canvases together into one group. Why? Because identity never stays at a stable room temperature. Studying in Chicago far from Beijing, my psychological state violently swings from frozen isolation to burning anxiety in a single afternoon. I attacked the canvas with thick oil strokes. I refuse to blend the edges smoothly. I want the raw color to punch your eyes. Do we actually look like the physical bodies in the mirror, or are we just shifting masses of emotional heat?

items.title: Sunlit Ripples
items.meta: **Year:** 2024  **Medium:** Oil on canvas  **Size:** 16 x 20 in
items.description: Bright yellow explodes against the deep blue water. Just pure light. I wanted to take a classic Chinese motif and inject it with the heavy, saturated energy of Western oil painting. The water ripples outward. Little red fish dart through the shadows. I am not painting a quiet, passive garden anymore. I am painting intense vitality. So I use thick, bright yellow strokes to make the leaves feel alive and muscular. They push right out of the dark water into the sun.

items.title: Domestic Uncanny(Triptych / View All Panels)
items.meta: Year: 2024  Medium: Oil on canvas (Triptych)  Size: 8 x 12 in (each panel)
items.description: In this triptych, I explore how internal isolation projects onto the neutral screen of everyday architecture. I treat these solitary spaces---a sterile restroom, a dim stairwell, a concrete facade---as receptive surfaces for the subconscious. The unsettling details are not objective realities, but materializations of inner fear: a hand clinging to the window sill from the outside, or a fleeting face in the mirror. I painted these scenes with a clinical flatness to heighten the contrast with these surreal intrusions. I attempt to visualize the dissonance of solitude, where the silence of a familiar domestic setting is interrupted by projections of fragility. The work suggests that the \"uncanny\" is not an external intruder, but a reflection of our own psyche. I seek to capture that vibration of anxiety where the safety of the home dissolves, and the walls themselves manifest our deepest terrors.

items.title: Walking Shells / Grant Park
items.meta: Year: 2025  Medium: Oil on canvas  Size: 10 x 30 in
items.description: Giant legs march across the bright green grass. But nobody has a head. I saw these massive sculptures in Chicago\'s Grant Park and felt an instant chill. They just keep walking forward. A blind, collective movement. Growing up in a huge city like Beijing, crowds usually felt like they had a shared, unified purpose. But here in the US, these heavy figures feel totally isolated even when they stand shoulder to shoulder. So I painted them but stripped away their original rust. I forced different weird textures onto their bodies. One is literally melting. Another is trapped in a rigid checkerboard pattern. I want to test how identity works when you completely remove the face. Do we just become the graphic patterns we wear? We walk. We occupy space. We just never really look at each other.

items.title: Staged Memory
items.meta: **Year:** 2024 **Medium:** Oil on canvas **Size:** 22 x 28 in
items.description: Dust floats in sharp beams of yellow light. The stage is bright. But she stands completely in the shadows. I painted a traditional Chinese theater to map the exact distance between a person and their own history. Growing up in Beijing, this culture surrounds you. Now in Chicago, I feel like a spectator of my own heritage. So I used thick oil paint to make the light beams feel like solid, physical barriers. The figure in the foreground wears heavy theatrical makeup. She is dressed for the part. And yet her gaze is totally detached. She is not looking at the performance. Is she an actor who stepped off the stage, or an audience member who got too close? We put our cultural memory under a spotlight. Do we actually live inside it, or do we just watch it perform for us in the dark?

items.meta: Year: 2024 Medium: Digital photograph / Self-Portrait Size: 16:9
items.title: Self-Portrait: The Chrysalis of Shadow
items.description: In this intimate self-portrait, I explore the boundary between containment and liberation. Submerged in dark, tranquil waters, I am depicted as an ethereal being—part human, part myth—finding solace in the shadows. The grand, textured wings contrast against the soft ripples of the water, symbolizing a quiet transformation or a state of dreaming. Bathed in a focal light emanating from my own hands, this piece is an introspection on inner illumination, embracing one's own mysteries, and the delicate beauty found within the dark.

items.title: Chromium Covenant: Iron and White
items.description:
1. Theme: Anachronism and Industrial Order
In this piece, I continue to visually investigate the concept of anachronism. I placed the subject, draped in translucent white classical fabric and adorned with botanical elements, within a dark, distinctly industrial environment. This is a visual collision between the fragile and the permanent. The figure is not a victim within this sterile space; through her posture and direct gaze, she anchors herself against this rigid, abandoned structure, forging a new myth within the industrial ruins.

2. Artistic Technique: Material Tension and Directional Light
I approach this image as a study of diametric opposites—absolute hardness versus absolute softness.

* Collision of Matter: The core physical focus is the extreme contrast between the matte, rough texture of the black cast-iron bar and the lightweight, light-transmitting white fabric. The iron represents insurmountable physical resistance, while the fabric and flora represent organic fluidity.
* Light as a Chisel: I utilized a single, high-intensity directional light (spotlight) to "sculpt" the frame. This light cuts through the darkness like a scalpel, forcibly extracting the figure from a background devoid of absolute depth. The high-contrast chiaroscuro erases extraneous environmental data, forcing the eye to focus on the material textures within the illuminated zones.
* Compositional Rigidity vs. Organic Form: The cast-iron bar establishes an absolutely horizontal, rigid dividing line across the center of the frame. The placement of the subject’s arm and the tilt of her torso disrupt this sterile horizontal, injecting human physical tension into what is otherwise a static mechanical structure.
items.meta: Year: 2024 Medium: Digital photograph
Year: 2023 Medium: Digital photograph
items.title: Night Drift
items.description:
1. Theme: Urban Interiority and the "Blue Hour"
Does the city illuminate us, or does it drown us? Night Drift investigates a psychological state of interiority amidst urban chaos. The subject’s downcast gaze rejects the spectator's eye, refusing to participate in the surrounding spectacle. This captures a specific modern loneliness inherent to the diaspora—a physical presence within the blinding metropolitan lights contrasted with an emotional detachment. While the frantic, golden energy of the water suggests the relentless rhythm of the external world, the figure remains a pool of stillness. This image functions not as a portrait of a person, but as the depiction of a barrier—the visualization of a silent wall constructed to shield thought from external noise.

2. Technique: Digital Pictorialism and Structural Mass
I treat the sensor not as a recording device, but as a canvas for subtractive synthesis.

Dissolution of Form: Utilizing an extremely shallow depth of field, I force the contextual background to disintegrate. The river lights are no longer rendered as objects but as pure bokeh textures—abstract brushstrokes of cyan and gold that mimic the broken mark-making of Late Impressionism. Reality is dissolved into atmosphere.

Compositional Anchoring: To prevent the image from becoming fragile due to its ethereal quality, the composition relies on heavy visual mass at the base. The rich, deep texture of the scarf and the negative space of the dark attire establish a solid pyramidal foundation. This tactile foreground forces a violent collision with the ephemeral, flickering background.

Color Tension: The palette deliberately heightens the complementary contrast between the melancholic cold blues of twilight shadows and the incandescent, electric warmth of artificial lighting. This conflict of color temperatures visualizes the tension between a cold, objective reality and the warm, subjective internal world.

items.title: Chromium Covenant
items.description:
### 1. Theme: Anachronism and the New Myth

History is frequently etched in stone—heavy, rigid, and historically exclusionary toward diverse identities. *Chromium Covenant* investigates the concept of anachronism, intentionally positioning elements outside of their traditional chronological context to trigger a visual collision.

The work juxtaposes the weight of Western Neoclassical architecture with the traditional symbolism of the sword, both representing ancient structures of power. This is disrupted by the central figure clad in futuristic liquid metal. Defined as a "time traveler," the subject subverts classical narratives. Rather than seeking permission to occupy this historical space, she claims agency by gripping the hilt. The theme focuses not on seamless integration, but on a dissonant beauty—the forging of a contemporary myth within the ruins of the old world.

### 2. Artistic Technique: Material Contrast and Sculptural Light

The visual execution functions as a study of extreme textural opposites, utilizing the sensor as a canvas to render distinct surfaces.

* Collision of Matter: The primary technical focus is the sharp contrast between the matte, weathered texture of the stone columns and the highly reflective, synthetic surface of the silver garment. While the stone absorbs light, the fabric repels it, generating a tactile tension throughout the frame.
* Light as a Chisel: Bright, diffused daylight is utilized to "sculpt" the figure. As light interacts with the metallic folds of the dress, the fabric adopts the appearance of molten lead or mercury. This technique imbues soft textile with the perceived rigidity of armor.
* Compositional Rigidity vs. Organic Form: The composition utilizes the solemn verticality of the architectural columns to establish a rigid grid. This geometric order is broken by the organic curves of the figure’s body and the tilt of her head, introducing essential human tension into the sterile, lithic environment.
items.meta: Medium: Digital photograph
Year: 2023 Medium: Digital photograph
items.title: Chromium Covenant: The Liquid Threshold
items.description:
1. Theme: Anachronism and the Artificial Threshold
In this piece, the "anachronism" manifests as a natural phenomenon physically isolated by an artificial landscape—the aquarium viewing window. I positioned the figure before a massive, pressurized glass interface, a threshold space connecting dried reality with a synthetic deep-sea environment. The visual conflict erupts between the cold precision of modern engineering and the primordial, fluid energy beneath the surface. Reclined in a near-meditative state, the figure does not merely observe; she utilizes this transparent physical barrier to perform a sensory reclamation of "encapsulated nature."

2. Artistic Technique: Acrylic Refraction and Spectral Spill
I treat this shoot as a study of the physical properties of light as it penetrates a heavy, artificial medium (acrylic).

* Collision of Matter: The frame is divided by the thick glass of the viewing window into two distinct density fields. The foreground consists of dry, concrete air and fabric; the background is a high-pressure, granular blue fluid. This physical spatiality, forcibly separated by industrial materials, creates a visual sense of deep-sea compression.
* Light Penetration and Refraction: I relied entirely on the artificial cool-blue light sources from within the tank. As light penetrates the thick transparent medium, it undergoes refraction and spills into the darkened viewing area in diffused, low-frequency wavelengths. This refracted light catches the edges of the gown's folds, granting the white fabric a visual illusion of bioluminescence.
* Geometric Confinement and Somatic Deconstruction: The circular viewport establishes the rigid boundary of the composition, symbolizing the human containment and observation of oceanic power. The diagonal organic curve of the subject’s body disrupts the perfect circularity, introducing an organic and unpredictable variable into this engineered framework.

items.title: Medical Astrology: The Hidden Flame Medium(Selection / View All Panels)
items.meta: Year: 2025  Title: : Ink on tracing paper, digital prints, lightbox  Size: 8.5 x 11 in (each)
items.description: This series reimagines the body as a sacred vessel. Based on Hermetic \"correspondence,\" I map Zodiac signs onto organs---visualizing the Aries ram in the brain. The work is a lightbox installation constructed from four stacked layers: (1) Exterior tattoos on tracing paper; (2) Skin photograph on bond paper; (3) Internal Zodiac tattoos on tracing paper; (4) Internal organ photograph. The experience transforms with light. OFF: Viewers see only the mundane outer skin. ON: Backlighting penetrates the layers, suddenly revealing the hidden organs and glowing \"inner tattoos\" beneath. This mechanism visualizes Gnostic revelation: light exposing the divine spark hidden within the flesh, turning anatomy into theology..

items.title: Pure Energy
items.meta: Year: 2026  Medium: Ink on paper  Size: 27 x 27 in
items.description: I washed away the meat. I only want the pure, vibrating energy underneath the biological body. The skin is a defensive lie. So I used fluid ink to paint a coiled snake skeleton. Just the spine. This strips the physical form down to its absolute core. I map the raw rhythm of life before it gets trapped inside a fleshy shell.
